---
title: "Automating GLP-1 Titration: A FHIR Deep Dive"
subtitle: "Production-Grade Clinical Decision Support"
author: "Naveed Pasha"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [dark, theme.scss]   # custom dark healthcare theme (see theme.scss)
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: default
    highlight-style: atom-one-dark
    width: 1200
    height: 675
    embed-resources: true       # produce a single self-contained HTML for easy recording/sharing
    incremental: false          # reveal lists explicitly with the {.incremental} class
    code-line-numbers: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    center: false
    progress: true
    controls: true
    history: true
    mermaid:
      theme: dark
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BUILD INSTRUCTIONS
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
#   Render to a standalone reveal.js HTML deck:
#       quarto render presentation.qmd
#   Live-reloading preview while editing:
#       quarto preview presentation.qmd
#   Export to PDF (for thumbnails / backup):
#       1) quarto preview presentation.qmd
#       2) open the deck, append ?print-pdf to the URL, then print -> Save as PDF
#       (or) quarto render presentation.qmd --to pdf   # requires a Chromium install
#
# RECORDING NOTES
#   - Output is a single self-contained HTML5 reveal.js deck.
#   - Capture in OBS / Loom at 1920x1080; the 1200x675 canvas scales cleanly to 16:9.
#   - Press "S" in the deck to open the speaker-notes window (notes are below each slide).
#   - Designed for ~90 s per slide => ~12 min narration across 10 slides + demo bridge.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
---

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 1 — TITLE
========================================================================= -->

## {.center data-menu-title="Title"}

::: {.center}
# Automating GLP-1 Titration {.r-fit-text}

### A FHIR Deep Dive

#### Production-Grade Clinical Decision Support {.muted}

[**Naveed Pasha**]{.big}

::: {.badges}
[Python]{.badge}
[FHIR R4]{.badge}
[US Core]{.badge}
[Clinical Automation]{.badge}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Hi everyone, and welcome. In this video we're going to look at how to automate
GLP-1 medication titration — the gradual dose escalation used in weight-management
programs — using the FHIR R4 healthcare interoperability standard.

This isn't a toy demo. We'll walk through a production-grade engine, written in
Python, that emits US Core–aligned FHIR resources you can drop straight into an EHR.
Whether you're a clinician curious about what automation can do, or a developer
who wants to see real FHIR resource generation, this talk is for you.

Let's get into it.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 2 — THE PROBLEM
========================================================================= -->

## The Problem {data-menu-title="The Problem"}

[Most GLP-1 weight-management programs still manage titration **manually**.]{.big .red}

::: {.incremental}
- 📊 [Manual spreadsheets]{.red} for patient tracking
- 💉 [Inconsistent dosing schedules]{.red} across clinicians
- 🚨 Adverse-event handling is [reactive, not automated]{.red}
- 🧩 EHR integration is [fragmented]{.red} and copy-paste driven
:::

::: {.callout-red .callout}
Manual ⇒ error-prone. Every spreadsheet edit is an unlogged clinical decision.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here's the problem. GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide have exploded in popularity for
weight management — but the operational side of running these programs is often
shockingly manual.

Clinics track patients in spreadsheets. Dosing schedules drift between providers.
When a patient calls in with severe nausea, somebody has to notice, remember the
protocol, and act — that's reactive, not systematic. And none of this is cleanly
wired into the EHR, so data gets re-keyed and lost.

For clinicians that means risk and wasted time. For developers that means brittle,
non-interoperable workflows. We can do much better — and FHIR is how.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 3 — CLINICAL BACKGROUND: WHAT IS TITRATION?
========================================================================= -->

## What Is GLP-1 Titration? {data-menu-title="Clinical Background"}

[**Titration** = gradual dose escalation for tolerability.]{.big}

Standard semaglutide initiation: [**0.25 mg once weekly**]{.green} for the first 4 weeks.

::: {.timeline}
[Week 1<br/>0.25 mg]{.week .week-std}
[➜]{.week-arrow}
[Week 2<br/>0.25 mg]{.week .week-std}
[➜]{.week-arrow}
[Week 3<br/>0.25 mg]{.week .week-std}
[➜]{.week-arrow}
[Week 4<br/>0.25 mg]{.week .week-std}
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Start [low to minimize GI side effects]{.green} (nausea, vomiting)
- Doses increase gradually along a [standard pathway]{.green}
- [Severe adverse events]{.orange} require provider review
- Patient compliance depends on [predictable dosing]{.green}
:::

::: {.source}
Source: FDA semaglutide label — 0.25 mg weekly is the standard initiation dose (not therapeutic; for GI tolerability).
:::

::: {.notes}
Quick clinical primer for the developers in the room, and a refresher for clinicians.

Titration just means easing into a medication by raising the dose gradually. GLP-1
receptor agonists hit the gut hard if you start too high — nausea and vomiting are
the big tolerability problems. So per the FDA label, semaglutide starts at 0.25 mg
once weekly. That first 0.25 mg phase isn't even a therapeutic weight-loss dose;
it exists purely to let the GI system adapt.

The key clinical rule we'll automate: most patients sail straight through, but if
someone reports a *severe* adverse event, you don't just keep escalating — you hold
and get a provider involved. Predictable, protocol-driven dosing is what keeps
patients compliant and safe.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 4 — WHAT IS FHIR?
========================================================================= -->

## What Is FHIR? Why Does It Matter? {data-menu-title="FHIR 101"}

::: {.callout .callout}
[**Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources**]{.blue} — the modern standard for
exchanging healthcare data. Think of it as [**the Bluetooth of healthcare**]{.fw-bold}:
standardized, plug-and-play communication between systems.
:::

::: {.cards}
::: {.card .card-blue}
[📋]{.card-icon}
[CarePlan]{.card-title .blue}
[The multi-week care pathway tying the whole titration together.]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-blue}
[💊]{.card-icon}
[MedicationRequest]{.card-title .blue}
[One weekly medication order per titration week.]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-blue}
[✅]{.card-icon}
[Task]{.card-title .blue}
[Provider alert raised when an adverse event needs triage.]{.card-body}
:::
:::

::: {.callout-green .callout}
FHIR lets hospitals integrate GLP-1 automation into existing EHR systems
[**without custom point-to-point code**]{.green}.
:::

::: {.notes}
So what is FHIR? It stands for Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources, and it's
the modern HL7 standard for moving health data around.

My favorite analogy: FHIR is the Bluetooth of healthcare. Before Bluetooth, every
device needed its own cable. FHIR is the standardized, plug-and-play layer so any
compliant system can speak the same language.

For this engine, three FHIR "resources" do the heavy lifting. A CarePlan is the
overall multi-week pathway. A MedicationRequest is a single weekly order — we'll
generate four of them. And a Task is a work item we hand to a provider when
something needs human attention.

The payoff: because these are standard resources, a hospital can plug this
automation into Epic, Cerner, or any FHIR server without bespoke integration code.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 5 — ENGINE ARCHITECTURE
========================================================================= -->

## How It Works: 3 Steps {data-menu-title="Architecture"}

::: {.flow}

::: {.step}
[1 · INPUT]{.step-label}
Patient demographics + initial weight + GLP-1 start date
:::

::: {.flow-arrow}
➜
:::

::: {.step}
[2 · LOGIC]{.step-label}
Compute the 4-week titration · detect severe adverse events · delay escalation if needed
:::

::: {.flow-arrow}
➜
:::

::: {.step}
[3 · OUTPUT]{.step-label}
A complete FHIR Bundle: CarePlan + MedicationRequests + Tasks
:::

:::

```python
from datetime import date
from src.engine.titration import GLP1TitrationEngine

engine = GLP1TitrationEngine(demographics, initial_weight, start_date=date(2026, 6, 2))
result = engine.generate_bundle()   # -> CarePlanEngineResult(.bundle, .task_ids, ...)
```

::: {.notes}
Architecturally the engine is dead simple — three steps, and it stays abstract
enough that both clinical and technical folks can follow it.

Step one, input: who's the patient, what's their starting weight, and when does
therapy begin. Step two, the logic: it computes the standard four-week 0.25 mg
schedule, and critically, it watches for severe adverse events — if it sees one,
it delays the later weeks and flags for provider review. Step three, output: it
emits a single FHIR transaction Bundle containing everything.

On the code side it's just as clean. You construct a GLP1TitrationEngine with the
inputs, call generate_bundle, and you get back a result object holding the FHIR
bundle plus metadata like the IDs of any provider tasks that were created. That's
the whole public surface.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 6 — STANDARD vs ADVERSE EVENT PATHWAYS  (the "wow" moment)
========================================================================= -->

## Two Scenarios {data-menu-title="Scenarios"}

::: {.compare}
::: {.pane-standard}
### ✅ Standard

[W1]{.week .week-std} → [W2]{.week .week-std} → [W3]{.week .week-std} → [W4]{.week .week-std}

**Outcome:** four 0.25 mg orders, no delays, no provider involvement.
:::

::: {.pane-adverse}
### 🚨 Severe Nausea

[W1]{.week .week-std} → [W2 HOLD]{.week .week-delay} → [W3 +7d]{.week .week-delay} → [W4 +7d]{.week .week-delay}

**Outcome:** same orders **+** delayed start dates **+** a provider `Task`.
:::
:::

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  A[Patient Input] --> B[Titration Logic]
  B --> C{Severe Adverse Event?}
  C -->|Yes| D[Delay Schedule + Create Task]
  C -->|No| E[Standard Schedule]
  D --> F[Generate FHIR Bundle]
  E --> F
```

::: {.callout-orange .callout}
When [**severe nausea**]{.orange} is detected, the engine automatically delays
subsequent week start dates by 7 days **and** creates a provider triage `Task`.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the moment that makes the whole thing worth building.

On the left, the happy path: a patient tolerates the drug fine. The engine produces
four weekly 0.25 mg orders, back to back, no delays, nobody paged.

On the right, the same patient reports severe nausea in week two. Watch what happens
automatically. The engine holds escalation, pushes the start dates of the remaining
weeks out by seven days, and — this is the safety-critical part — it generates a
FHIR Task assigned to a weight-management clinician with urgent priority.

So the decision tree at the bottom is the entire clinical brain: input, compute,
ask "was there a severe adverse event?", and branch. No human had to remember the
protocol. The system encodes it, every single time, with a full audit trail.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 7 — THE FHIR BUNDLE OUTPUT
========================================================================= -->

## What Does the Output Look Like? {data-menu-title="Bundle Output"}

```{.plaintext .tree}
Bundle (transaction)
├── 👤 Patient                 (US Core, demographics + MRN)
├── ⚖️  Observation            (body weight · LOINC 29463-7)
├── 📋 CarePlan                (active · weight-management)
├── 💊 MedicationRequest       (Week 1 · semaglutide 0.25 mg)
├── 💊 MedicationRequest       (Week 2)
├── 💊 MedicationRequest       (Week 3)
├── 💊 MedicationRequest       (Week 4)
└── 🚨 Task + Observation      (provider triage — only if adverse event)
```

::: {.fragment}
```json
{
  "resourceType": "MedicationRequest",
  "status": "active",
  "intent": "order",
  "medicationCodeableConcept": {
    "coding": [{
      "system": "http://www.nlm.nih.gov/research/umls/rxnorm",
      "code": "1991302",
      "display": "semaglutide 0.25 MG/0.37 ML Pen Injector"
    }]
  },
  "dosageInstruction": [{ "text": "Inject 0.25 mg subcutaneously once weekly — titration week 1" }]
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-green .callout}
Every resource carries a US Core profile and coded terminology — [**ready for any FHIR-compliant EHR**]{.green}.
:::

::: {.notes}
Here's the deliverable: a single FHIR transaction Bundle. At the top is the patient,
then a body-weight Observation coded with LOINC, then the CarePlan that ties it all
together, then the four weekly MedicationRequests. And if there was a severe adverse
event, you also get the provider Task plus an adverse-event Observation appended.

The JSON below is a real, trimmed MedicationRequest. Notice it's not hand-rolled
strings — the medication is coded with RxNorm code 1991302, the actual semaglutide
0.25 mg pen injector, and the dosage instruction reads like a real prescription.

Crucially, every resource is stamped with a US Core profile. That's the US national
implementation guide for FHIR. So this bundle isn't just valid FHIR — it's the
flavor of FHIR that American EHRs actually expect. We'll see the full JSON live in
the demo.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 8 — LIVE DEMO BRIDGE  (transition to screen share)
========================================================================= -->

## Let's See It In Action {.dimmed data-menu-title="Demo Bridge"}

```python
from datetime import date
from src.engine.titration import GLP1TitrationEngine
from src.models.fhir_helpers import PatientDemographics, WeightObservationInput

engine = GLP1TitrationEngine(
    demographics=PatientDemographics(
        family_name="Rivera", given_names=["Alex"],
        birth_date=date(1985, 3, 14), gender="female", mrn="MRN-10042",
    ),
    initial_weight=WeightObservationInput(value_kg=98.4, observed_on=date(2026, 6, 1)),
    start_date=date(2026, 6, 2),
)
result = engine.generate_bundle()
```

::: {.center}
[⬇️ In the next segment, we'll run this **live**.]{.big .orange}
:::

<!--
============================================================================
>>> SCREEN-SHARE / LIVE DEMO BEGINS HERE <<<
The slide deck (Part 1) ends on this slide. Stop the slide capture and switch
your recording source to the terminal/editor screen share. Suggested live flow:
  1. Show the project layout and `pip install -e ".[dev]"`.
  2. Run the engine code above in a Python REPL; pretty-print result.bundle.
  3. Run `python3 scripts/generate_samples.py` to produce both sample bundles.
  4. Re-run with apply_adverse_event(...) to show the Task + delayed dates.
  5. Optionally validate the bundle against a public FHIR validator.
============================================================================
-->

::: {.notes}
Okay — this is the bridge into the live demo. On screen is the exact code we'll run.
We build a patient, Alex Rivera, give a starting weight of 98.4 kilograms and a
start date, then call generate_bundle.

I've dimmed this slide on purpose to signal the transition. At this point in the
recording I'll stop the slide capture and switch over to a screen share of my
terminal and editor.

In that next segment we'll install the package, run this exact snippet live,
pretty-print the resulting FHIR bundle, generate the sample files, and then inject
a severe adverse event so you can watch the Task and the delayed dates appear in
real time. Let's switch over.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 9 — KEY TAKEAWAYS
========================================================================= -->

## Three Takeaways {data-menu-title="Takeaways"}

::: {.cards}
::: {.card .card-green}
[✅]{.card-icon}
[Less manual work]{.card-title .green}
[GLP-1 automation cuts spreadsheet busywork and improves compliance.]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-green}
[✅]{.card-icon}
[EHR-ready by design]{.card-title .green}
[FHIR standards enable EHR integration without custom engineering.]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-green}
[✅]{.card-icon}
[Production-ready]{.card-title .green}
[Clinically modeled and US Core aligned — not a prototype.]{.card-body}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Three things to take away.

One: automation removes the manual, spreadsheet-driven busywork and makes dosing
consistent, which directly helps patient compliance and safety.

Two: because we built on FHIR and US Core, this slots into existing EHR systems
without a custom integration project for every site.

Three: this is production-grade. The clinical logic — the four-week ladder, the
severe-event hold, the provider task — is modeled deliberately and the output is
US Core aligned. It's built to be deployed, not just demoed.
:::

<!-- ========================================================================
SLIDE 10 — CALL TO ACTION
========================================================================= -->

## Next Steps {data-menu-title="Next Steps"}

::: {.cards}
::: {.card .card-blue}
[⭐]{.card-icon}
[Get the code]{.card-title .blue}
[github.com/naveedpash/<br/>clinical-careplan-automation]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-green}
[⚙️]{.card-icon}
[Try it]{.card-title .green}
[`pip install -e ".[dev]"`<br/>then `pytest tests/ -v`]{.card-body}
:::
::: {.card .card-orange}
[▶️]{.card-icon}
[Watch Part 2]{.card-title .orange}
[Live demo & full code walkthrough]{.card-body}
:::
:::

::: {.center}
[**Questions? Comments? Subscribe for Part 2!**]{.big}

[Scan / visit: github.com/naveedpash/clinical-careplan-automation]{.muted .small}
:::

<!-- Optional: drop a QR code PNG here that links to the repo, e.g.
     ![Repo QR code](images/repo-qr.png){fig-alt="QR code linking to the GitHub repository" width="160"}
     Generate with: `qrencode -o images/repo-qr.png https://github.com/naveedpash/clinical-careplan-automation`
-->

::: {.notes}
That's the deck. If you want to dig in, the full source is on GitHub at
naveedpash slash clinical-careplan-automation. Cloning it and running pip install
dash e with the dev extras, then pytest, gets you a green test suite in under a
minute.

Part two is the live demo and full code walkthrough — that's the screen-share
segment. If this was useful, subscribe so you catch it, and drop any questions in
the comments. Thanks for watching.
:::
